items.title: Table design and normalization
items.description: Design OLTP tables to at least Third Normal Form (3NF) so each non-key column depends on the key, the whole key, and nothing but the key; this eliminates update anomalies and redundant storage. Normalize first, then denormalize deliberately and only where a measured read pattern justifies it, documenting the trade-off. Choose narrow, fixed-width, NOT NULL columns where possible because they keep rows compact, fit more rows per 8 KB page, and reduce I/O. Avoid wide nullable VARCHAR sprawl and 'one big table' designs that conflate multiple entities.
items.applicability: Applies to all transactional schema design in MSSQL; reporting/warehouse stars are a separate, intentionally denormalized case.
items.example: Split a Customer table holding repeated address fields into Customer and Address (1:N) so a customer's third address is a row, not columns Address1/Address2/Address3. Prefer DATE/DATETIME2 over storing dates as VARCHAR.
items.limitation: Over-normalization forces many joins on hot read paths; balance against query cost rather than pursuing normal forms as an end in themselves.

items.title: Primary keys: surrogate vs natural
items.description: A surrogate key (typically an IDENTITY INT/BIGINT or a sequence) is system-generated and carries no business meaning, so it never changes and joins stay cheap; a natural key is a real-world attribute (account number, ISO code) that is meaningful but can change or be reused. The default for OLTP is a narrow surrogate clustered primary key plus a UNIQUE constraint to enforce the natural key's business uniqueness. Prefer INT/BIGINT IDENTITY over random GUIDs as the clustered key, because a random uniqueidentifier causes page splits and index fragmentation from non-sequential inserts.
items.applicability: Use for entity tables needing stable, join-friendly identity; natural keys still belong as enforced UNIQUE constraints.
items.example: CREATE TABLE Account (AccountId INT IDENTITY(1,1) CONSTRAINT PK_Account PRIMARY KEY, AccountNumber VARCHAR(20) NOT NULL CONSTRAINT UQ_Account_Number UNIQUE, ...). If a GUID is required, use NEWSEQUENTIALID() default or keep the GUID non-clustered.
items.limitation: Surrogates can mask duplicate business rows if the natural UNIQUE constraint is omitted; always enforce both.

items.title: Foreign keys and referential integrity
items.description: Foreign keys enforce referential integrity at the engine level so orphaned child rows cannot exist, and they give the optimizer trusted relationship metadata it uses for join elimination and better cardinality estimates. Always index the foreign key column on the child side, because SQL Server does not create that index automatically and unindexed FKs cause table scans on the child during parent deletes and on lookups. Choose ON DELETE behavior deliberately: NO ACTION (block), CASCADE (propagate), or SET NULL; cascading chains can fan out into large, slow, lock-heavy operations.
items.applicability: Apply to every parent-child relationship; relax only for high-volume staging/ETL landing tables where FKs are validated downstream.
items.example: ALTER TABLE OrderLine ADD CONSTRAINT FK_OrderLine_Order FOREIGN KEY (OrderId) REFERENCES [Order](OrderId); CREATE INDEX IX_OrderLine_OrderId ON OrderLine(OrderId);
items.limitation: FKs add validation cost on writes and can complicate bulk loads; a NOT TRUSTED constraint (after WITH NOCHECK) is ignored by the optimizer until re-validated with WITH CHECK CHECK.

items.title: Index design and selectivity
items.description: Indexes trade write cost and storage for read speed; an index helps only when it is selective enough that a seek beats a scan (the optimizer favors scans once the estimated row count crosses a tipping point). The clustered index defines physical row order and should be narrow, unique, static, and ideally ever-increasing; every non-clustered index implicitly carries the clustering key as its row locator. Use covering indexes with INCLUDE columns to satisfy a query entirely from the index and avoid expensive key lookups, and put the most selective, equality-predicate column first in a composite key. Watch for low-selectivity columns (bit/status flags) where a filtered index is often better than a full index.
items.applicability: Apply when tuning read-heavy queries; review missing-index DMVs and the plan cache before adding indexes, and prune unused/duplicate indexes.
items.example: Covering: CREATE INDEX IX_Order_Cust_Date ON [Order](CustomerId, OrderDate) INCLUDE(Status, Total); Filtered: CREATE INDEX IX_Order_Open ON [Order](OrderDate) WHERE Status = 'Open';
items.limitation: Each extra index slows INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE and bloats storage; missing-index DMV suggestions are naive and must be consolidated, not applied verbatim.

items.title: Views vs inline queries
items.description: A view is a named, reusable SELECT that centralizes a query definition and can restrict column/row exposure for security; it is logical and adds no inherent performance benefit because the engine expands and optimizes it inline at runtime. Nested views (views referencing views) frequently produce poor plans and over-joined queries that read columns no caller needs. For materialized acceleration use an indexed view (with SCHEMABINDING and a unique clustered index), which stores results but imposes strict requirements and write-time maintenance cost. In application code, prefer parameterized inline queries or stored procedures for hot paths so the optimizer sees only the columns and predicates actually required.
items.applicability: Use views for shared, stable projections and access control; avoid deep view nesting and SELECT * over views on performance-critical paths.
items.example: CREATE VIEW vw_ActiveCustomer WITH SCHEMABINDING AS SELECT CustomerId, Name FROM dbo.Customer WHERE IsActive = 1; an indexed view additionally needs CREATE UNIQUE CLUSTERED INDEX on it.
items.limitation: Indexed views require deterministic, SCHEMABINDING-compatible definitions and slow down base-table writes; they are not a free cache.

items.title: Stored procedure patterns
items.description: Stored procedures encapsulate set-based logic close to the data, enable plan reuse, and form a permission boundary (grant EXEC without granting table access). Begin each procedure with SET NOCOUNT ON to suppress done-in-proc messages, use explicit transactions with structured TRY/CATCH and THROW for error handling, and qualify objects with schema (dbo.) to avoid name-resolution overhead. Beware parameter sniffing: a plan compiled for one parameter value can be wrong for another, causing skewed performance; mitigate with OPTION (RECOMPILE), OPTIMIZE FOR, or local-variable patterns only where measured. Keep logic set-based; avoid row-by-row cursors and per-row scalar UDF calls.
items.applicability: Use for transactional write paths, batch operations, and any logic needing a security boundary or plan reuse.
items.example: CREATE PROC dbo.usp_PlaceOrder @CustomerId INT AS BEGIN SET NOCOUNT ON; BEGIN TRY BEGIN TRAN; INSERT ...; COMMIT; END TRY BEGIN CATCH IF @@TRANCOUNT>0 ROLLBACK; THROW; END CATCH END;
items.limitation: Procedures can hide a parameter-sniffing problem behind a cached plan; OPTION (RECOMPILE) fixes estimates but adds per-execution compile cost.

items.title: Trigger cautions
items.description: DML triggers run inside the firing statement's transaction, so any work they do extends lock duration and can amplify blocking and deadlocks; they also hide side effects from developers reading only the calling statement. Critically, a trigger fires once per statement, not once per row, so its body must be written set-based against the inserted and deleted pseudo-tables — code assuming a single row silently corrupts data on multi-row operations. Avoid long-running work, external calls, or cross-database writes in triggers, and never assume execution order among multiple triggers on the same table. Prefer constraints, computed columns, or explicit procedure logic when they can do the job.
items.applicability: Reserve triggers for cross-cutting concerns like audit capture where application code cannot reliably enforce them; otherwise prefer declarative alternatives.
items.example: Correct set-based audit: INSERT INTO Audit(Key, NewVal) SELECT i.Id, i.Val FROM inserted i; -- never SELECT @v = Val FROM inserted (breaks on multi-row updates).
items.limitation: Triggers are easy to overlook during debugging and can quietly break batch updates; they also block TRUNCATE-style fast paths.

items.title: Transactions and ACID
items.description: A transaction is the unit of work guaranteeing ACID: Atomicity (all-or-nothing), Consistency (constraints hold across the boundary), Isolation (concurrent transactions do not corrupt each other), and Durability (committed changes survive crashes via the write-ahead log). Keep transactions short and touch the fewest rows possible, because open transactions hold locks and pin the log, growing the active portion that cannot be truncated. Never leave a transaction open across user input or a network round-trip, and pair BEGIN TRAN with disciplined COMMIT/ROLLBACK plus @@TRANCOUNT checks. Use XACT_ABORT ON so a runtime error reliably aborts and rolls back rather than leaving a partial, lock-holding transaction.
items.applicability: Wrap multi-statement writes that must succeed or fail as a unit; single autocommit statements are already atomic and need no explicit transaction.
items.example: SET XACT_ABORT ON; BEGIN TRAN; UPDATE Account SET Bal=Bal-@amt WHERE Id=@from; UPDATE Account SET Bal=Bal+@amt WHERE Id=@to; COMMIT;
items.limitation: Long or chatty transactions block other sessions and bloat the transaction log; nesting only increments @@TRANCOUNT and does not create true save-point independence (use SAVE TRAN for that).

items.title: Isolation levels
items.description: Isolation level trades concurrency against consistency anomalies: READ UNCOMMITTED allows dirty reads, the default READ COMMITTED prevents dirty reads but allows non-repeatable reads and phantoms, REPEATABLE READ holds shared locks, and SERIALIZABLE prevents phantoms via range locks at the cost of concurrency. Read Committed Snapshot Isolation (RCSI), enabled per database with ALTER DATABASE SET READ_COMMITTED_SNAPSHOT ON, makes readers use row-versioned snapshots from tempdb so readers do not block writers and writers do not block readers — usually the best default for OLTP. The widespread WITH (NOLOCK) hint is READ UNCOMMITTED and is dangerous: it can return uncommitted, duplicated, or skipped rows during page splits, so it must not be used where correctness matters (e.g., financial totals). Prefer enabling RCSI over scattering NOLOCK hints.
items.applicability: Choose per workload; enable RCSI for read-heavy OLTP to cut blocking, and reserve SERIALIZABLE for operations requiring strict phantom protection.
items.example: ALTER DATABASE [App] SET READ_COMMITTED_SNAPSHOT ON; -- then normal queries get non-blocking versioned reads. Avoid: SELECT SUM(Amount) FROM Ledger WITH (NOLOCK).
items.limitation: RCSI shifts load to tempdb (version store) and adds 14 bytes per row; snapshot reads can serve slightly stale committed data, which some workflows cannot tolerate.

items.title: Deadlock causes and avoidance
items.description: A deadlock occurs when two sessions each hold a lock the other needs, forming a cycle; SQL Server's monitor detects it and kills the lower-cost session as the deadlock victim (error 1205). The most common root cause is inconsistent lock acquisition order across code paths, often worsened by long transactions, missing indexes that force scans (escalating lock footprint), and lock escalation from many row locks to a table lock. Mitigate by accessing tables in a consistent order everywhere, keeping transactions short, covering the queries that participate so they seek rather than scan, and using RCSI to remove reader/writer conflicts. Capture the deadlock graph via the system_health Extended Events session or a deadlock trace to see the exact resources and statements involved, then make the application idempotent and retry on 1205.
items.applicability: Apply when error 1205 appears under concurrency; combine query/index tuning with consistent ordering and a bounded retry loop.
items.example: Read the graph: SELECT XEvent FROM sys.fn_xe_file_target_read_file('system_health*.xel',NULL,NULL,NULL); fix by ordering UPDATEs Account-then-Ledger in every proc, and retry the transaction up to 3 times on error 1205.
items.limitation: Retries assume the transaction is safely re-runnable; blind retries on non-idempotent logic can double-apply effects, and RCSI does not prevent write/write deadlocks.

items.title: Audit and history tables
items.description: Audit/history tables preserve who changed what and when for compliance, debugging, and point-in-time reconstruction — essential in regulated domains like banking middleware. Capture is typically append-only (no UPDATE/DELETE on the audit table) and records the actor, UTC timestamp, operation, and before/after values; treating the audit log as immutable is what makes it trustworthy. SQL Server offers system-versioned temporal tables (WITH SYSTEM_VERSIONING) that automatically maintain a paired history table and enable FOR SYSTEM_TIME AS OF queries, which is cleaner and less error-prone than hand-rolled audit triggers. Always store timestamps in UTC and index the history table on the entity key plus the period column for efficient as-of retrieval. This complements the platform's append-only audit principle used for security events.
items.applicability: Use for entities with compliance, traceability, or rollback requirements; prefer temporal tables when on a supporting SQL Server version and the schema can carry period columns.
items.example: CREATE TABLE Account (... PERIOD FOR SYSTEM_TIME (ValidFrom, ValidTo)) WITH (SYSTEM_VERSIONING = ON (HISTORY_TABLE = dbo.AccountHistory)); query: SELECT * FROM Account FOR SYSTEM_TIME AS OF '2026-01-01' WHERE Id=@id;
items.limitation: History tables grow continuously and need retention/archival policy; temporal tables impose schema constraints (period columns, primary key) and complicate some schema migrations.

items.title: Backup and restore (with restore testing)
items.description: A backup strategy must be defined by the recovery objectives (RPO/RTO) and the database recovery model: SIMPLE recovery truncates the log and forbids log backups (RPO limited to the last full/differential), while FULL recovery with periodic log backups enables point-in-time restore. A typical FULL-recovery plan combines weekly full, daily differential, and frequent (e.g., 15-minute) transaction-log backups; log backups also keep the log from growing unbounded. The cardinal rule is that an untested backup is not a backup — schedule regular restore drills, verify with RESTORE VERIFYONLY and CHECKSUM, and run DBCC CHECKDB on restored copies to confirm integrity. Store backups off the source server/media and protect them, since they contain the full data set.
items.applicability: Apply to every production database; choose recovery model and cadence from the tolerated data-loss window and required restore speed.
items.example: BACKUP DATABASE [App] TO DISK='...full.bak' WITH CHECKSUM, COMPRESSION; BACKUP LOG [App] TO DISK='...log.trn'; periodically: RESTORE DATABASE [App_test] FROM DISK='...full.bak' WITH MOVE..., RECOVERY; then DBCC CHECKDB([App_test]).
items.limitation: SIMPLE recovery cannot do point-in-time or log-tail restore; backup compression/encryption add CPU, and a corrupt-but-unverified backup chain is discovered only at restore time — too late.

items.title: Query performance and execution plans
items.description: Diagnose slow queries by reading the actual execution plan (not just the estimated one) and looking for scans where seeks are expected, large estimated-vs-actual row discrepancies (stale or missing statistics), expensive key lookups, spills to tempdb, and warning icons. Write SARGable predicates so indexes are usable: avoid wrapping the indexed column in functions (WHERE YEAR(d)=2026 defeats the index; use a range instead) and avoid implicit conversions from mismatched data types. Keep statistics current and rebuild/reorganize indexes by fragmentation level, and capture real workloads with the Query Store to find regressed plans and force a good one. Per the platform's reliability rules, prefer simple, predictable queries (separate COUNT(*) calls) and avoid clever aggregate projections such as GroupBy on a constant key, which have produced indefinite page hangs on SQL Server.
items.applicability: Apply when a query or page is slow or hangs; start from the actual plan and the Query Store, and validate predicates are SARGable before adding indexes.
items.example: Non-SARGable: WHERE CONVERT(date, OrderDate) = '2026-06-20'. SARGable: WHERE OrderDate >= '2026-06-20' AND OrderDate < '2026-06-21'. Avoid GROUP BY (SELECT 1) style aggregate-over-constant projections; issue separate COUNT(*) queries instead.
items.limitation: Plans depend on parameter values and statistics, so a plan captured once may not represent production; forcing a plan can mask an underlying indexing or statistics problem.
